channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Topic
channel.description: Uplifting stories from around the world, organised by topic.

item.title: UK Electric Cars Undercut Petrol Prices for the Very First Time
item.description: The average new electric vehicle in Britain now costs £42,620 — £785 less than the average new petrol car — marking a historic milestone driven by sustained manufacturer discounts and government grants. Electric vehicles accounted for 22% of new car registrations in Q1 2026, up 14.5% year-on-year.

item.title: Laos–China Railway Clocks 800,000 Cross-Border Journeys on Its Third Anniversary
item.description: Three years after its cross-border passenger launch, the Laos–China Railway has carried more than 800,000 international travelers between Vientiane and Kunming — a journey now completed in under 10 hours instead of several days. Tourism corridors have widened and freight has surged, reshaping Southeast Asian connectivity.

item.title: African Startups Raise a Record $705 Million in Q1 2026, Up 27%
item.description: Across 59 deals in the first quarter, African tech startups pulled in $705 million — a 26.5% jump year-on-year — with Egypt, South Africa and Kenya leading the charge. Google’s 10th Africa Accelerator cohort, chosen from 2,600 applicants, spotlights AI-first founders tackling local challenges.

item.title: Printed Artificial Neurons Successfully Communicate With Living Brain Cells
item.description: Northwestern University engineers used aerosol jet printing to create flexible, ink-based artificial neurons that generate lifelike electrical signals and trigger real responses in mouse brain tissue. Published in Nature Nanotechnology, the breakthrough opens a path toward brain-machine interfaces that could restore hearing, vision and movement.

item.title: New AI Architecture Slashes Energy Use 100-Fold While Boosting Accuracy
item.description: Researchers unveiled a hybrid AI design combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning — mirroring how humans break problems into steps — achieving a 100-fold reduction in energy consumption alongside improved accuracy. The approach could dramatically shrink the carbon footprint of large-scale AI systems.

item.title: Scientists Stumble Upon an Uncharted Island in Antarctica’s Weddell Sea
item.description: While sheltering from a storm in the Weddell Sea, 93 scientists aboard Germany’s Polarstern icebreaker discovered a previously unmapped island — 130 metres long and 16 metres above sea level — whose ice-covered surface had fooled satellites for decades. The expedition team will officially name the new island.

item.title: Golden Eagles Set to Soar Over England Again After 150 Years Away
item.description: The UK government has committed £1 million to explore reintroducing golden eagles to England, where Victorian-era persecution wiped them out entirely. Eight potential recovery zones across northern England have been identified, and satellite-tagged birds from Scotland have already begun scouting the border regions.

item.title: European Bison Rebounds From Near-Extinction to Over 9,000 Animals
item.description: A century after just 60 individuals survived in captivity, the European bison population has climbed above 9,000 free-roaming animals — a tenfold increase in a decade. A new study confirms the herds now sequester carbon equivalent to offsetting 84,000 petrol cars each year.

item.title: New Zealand Launches Bold ‘Action for Nature’ Plan to Restore Wildlife
item.description: Conservation Minister Tama Potaka unveiled the ‘Action for Nature’ implementation plan on April 23, setting a clear framework for smarter investment, stronger community partnerships and targeted protection of the species and habitats that need help most. The plan aims to maximise every conservation dollar’s impact.

item.title: Algeria Becomes the 29th Country to Eradicate Infectious Blindness
item.description: The World Health Organization has validated Algeria as the 29th nation to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem — the world’s leading cause of infectious blindness. The achievement caps decades of community health work reaching millions with antibiotics and hygiene education.

item.title: Cornell Scientists Take a Giant Step Toward Reversible Male Birth Control
item.description: A Cornell University study shows a small molecule targeting a checkpoint in sperm-cell division completely halts sperm production in mice, with full fertility restored within six weeks of stopping treatment. The nonhormonal approach sidesteps the side effects that stalled every previous male contraceptive effort.

item.title: Accelerated TMS Therapy Puts Severe Depression Into Rapid Remission
item.description: A study from the Medical University of South Carolina shows the accelerated SAINT transcranial magnetic stimulation protocol achieves remission rates for severe depression far higher than conventional treatments, with patients recovering quickly enough to reengage with work and daily life within days of completing therapy.

item.title: Zambia Ends Bail for Sexual Offence Suspects to Protect Survivors
item.description: Zambia’s Criminal Procedure Code Amendment Act now makes sexual offences including rape and child abuse non-bailable, requiring suspects to remain in custody until trial. Justice Minister Princess Kasune Zulu, a child-marriage survivor turned activist, championed the reform as a landmark step toward justice.

item.title: Chile on Track to Protect Over Half Its Waters, Surpassing Global Target
item.description: Chile is set to bring more than 50% of its territorial waters under formal protection — nearly double the 30-by-2030 global target that 190 nations pledged to pursue. The commitment safeguards extraordinary marine biodiversity across some of the most pristine ocean in the Southern Hemisphere.

item.title: Pacific Island Nations Unite Around 100% Renewable Vision in Tassiriki Call
item.description: Pacific Island states have issued the Tassiriki Call — a landmark unified declaration backing 100% renewable energy and an urgent fossil fuel phase-out ahead of a major international conference in Colombia. The declaration amplifies the Pacific’s moral authority as the world’s most climate-frontline communities.

item.title: Peabody Awards Honor Films on Musical Geniuses Sly Stone and Fela Kuti
item.description: The 86th Peabody Awards recognised two powerful arts programs celebrating Black musical pioneers: Questlove’s documentary ‘Sly Lives! (aka The Burden of Black Genius)’ and Jad Abumrad’s podcast ‘Fela Kuti: Fear No Man’. Both explore the personal costs and enduring cultural legacies of transformative innovators.

item.title: Coachella 2026 Sells Out in Days as Latin Megastar Karol G Headlines
item.description: Passes for both Coachella 2026 weekends sold out in just two days, driven by headliners including Colombian reggaeton superstar and Grammy winner Karol G alongside Justin Bieber and Sabrina Carpenter. Dazzling new art installations cemented this edition as a landmark global celebration of music and culture.

item.title: Indigenous Filmmaker Gail Maurice Wins imagineNATIVE’s Prestigious Augie Award
item.description: The imagineNATIVE Film + Media Arts Festival presented Métis director, actor and writer Gail Maurice with the 2026 August Schellenberg ‘Augie’ Award for Excellence — honouring her career-long contributions to Indigenous storytelling and deep commitment to mentoring emerging voices across Turtle Island.

item.title: Michigan Wolverines Win 2026 NCAA Title, Ending a 37-Year Championship Drought
item.description: The University of Michigan men’s basketball team defeated UConn 69–63 to claim the 2026 national championship — the program’s first title since 1989. They posted the highest net rating of any champion in KenPom history, ending a 37-year wait for jubilant fans.

item.title: Zhao Xintong Opens World Title Defence With Stunning Form After Comeback
item.description: Defending snooker world champion Zhao Xintong opened the 2026 World Championship at the Crucible with a 10–7 first-round win, continuing an electrifying season in which he swept three ranking titles. His return from a 20-month match-fixing suspension has become one of sport’s most compelling comebacks.

item.title: Denver Pioneers Claim Their Record 11th NCAA Men’s Ice Hockey Crown
item.description: The University of Denver Pioneers defeated Wisconsin 2–1 on April 12 to claim the 2026 NCAA Division I Men’s Ice Hockey championship — their record 11th title. The win reaffirmed Denver’s status as the undisputed gold standard of college hockey.

item.title: Renewables Overtake Coal in Global Power Mix for First Time Since 1919
item.description: Ember's Global Electricity Review 2026 confirmed a landmark: renewable energy — powered by a record 30% surge in solar — overtook coal to supply more than a third of global electricity in 2025, for the first time in over a century. Solar alone met 75% of global electricity demand growth and, together with wind, met almost all new demand. It marked the first year this century that total fossil fuel generation did not rise — falling by 38 TWh.

item.title: Japan Backs Homegrown AI Chip Pioneer Rapidus as Microsoft Pledges $10B
item.description: Japan approved ¥631.5 billion in additional subsidies for domestic AI chipmaker Rapidus after inspectors signed off on the startup's technical progress at its Hokkaido foundry. The boost arrives alongside Microsoft's commitment to invest $10 billion in Japanese AI infrastructure and workforce training between 2026 and 2029. Together these investments are propelling Japan's ambition to hold a 30% global share of the physical AI market by 2040.

item.title: Vietnam Leads ASEAN With a 7.83% Economic Surge in Early 2026
item.description: Vietnam's economy grew 7.83% year-on-year in Q1 2026 — the fastest rate in Southeast Asia and well above government targets. Strong manufacturing exports and rising domestic consumption drove the expansion, with the National Statistics Office calling it one of the best first-quarter performances in the country's recent history. The result cements Vietnam's standing as one of the Asia-Pacific's most dynamic emerging economies.

item.title: Bowel Cancer Trial Reports Zero Relapses After Nearly Three Years
item.description: The NEOPRISM-CRC trial, which gave 32 high-risk bowel cancer patients pembrolizumab immunotherapy before surgery, recorded zero relapses after 33 months of follow-up — a result that astonished oncologists. Initially 59% of patients showed no signs of disease after treatment; even those with residual cancer saw no growth or spread during the monitoring period. UCL researchers also developed personalised blood tests to predict each patient's response, pointing the way toward truly tailored cancer therapy.

item.title: Pink Antarctic Boulders Lead Scientists to a Hidden Giant Beneath a Glacier
item.description: Geologists solved a long-standing Antarctic puzzle when they traced mysterious Jurassic-age pink granite boulders in the Hudson Mountains back to their source: a massive subglacial rock formation buried beneath Pine Island Glacier, nearly 100 km wide and 7 km thick. By matching the boulders' age with gravity anomalies detected from aircraft, researchers confirmed the previously unknown feature. The discovery reshapes understanding of how bedrock geology influences the stability of one of Antarctica's fastest-retreating glaciers.

item.title: Scientists Drill Record 228-Metre Core From Beneath Antarctica's Ice Sheet
item.description: A team co-led by Imperial College London extracted the longest sediment core ever drilled from beneath an Antarctic ice sheet — a 228-metre column of ancient mud and rock spanning 23 million years of climate history. Researchers bored through 523 metres of ice at the remote Crary Ice Rise site to reach the sediment, finding the first in-situ evidence of open-ocean conditions in West Antarctica's interior. The core will help scientists understand how past warming reshaped the ice sheet and predict future sea-level rise.

item.title: Amazon Deforestation Drops 35%, Placing Brazil on Track for Historic Low
item.description: Satellite data shows Amazon deforestation fell 35% between August 2025 and January 2026 compared to the prior year, reaching its lowest level since 2014. If the trend holds through the year, Environment Minister Marina Silva says 2026 could mark the lowest annual deforestation rate since records began in 1988. Stronger enforcement and the participation of 70 high-deforestation municipalities in federal prevention programmes are credited with driving the change.

item.title: Kenya Opens World's Largest Black Rhino Sanctuary at Tsavo West
item.description: Kenya unveiled the expanded Tsavo West Rhino Sanctuary in December 2025, stretching across 3,200 square kilometres — the largest rhino sanctuary on Earth. The site now shelters 200 black rhinos, the biggest single population in Kenya, and positions the country as guardian of 78% of the world's Eastern black rhinos. By 2030, the sanctuary is expected to generate over $45 million a year in conservation tourism.

item.title: Europe's Electric Vehicle Sales Surge 29% in Q1 — 51% in March Alone
item.description: Sales of electric vehicles across Europe rose 29.4% in Q1 2026 compared with the same period last year, with growth accelerating to 51.3% in March — the fastest monthly increase on record. New affordable models and expanded charging infrastructure are fuelling the boom, with multiple markets hitting historic market-share highs. Europe's EV momentum is seen as a major tailwind for the continent's climate and clean air targets.

item.title: Bowel Cancer Trial Reports Zero Relapses After Nearly Three Years
item.description: The NEOPRISM-CRC trial, which gave 32 high-risk bowel cancer patients pembrolizumab immunotherapy before surgery, recorded zero relapses after 33 months of follow-up — a result that astonished oncologists. Initially 59% of patients showed no signs of disease after treatment; even those with residual cancer saw no growth or spread during the monitoring period. UCL researchers also developed personalised blood tests to predict each patient's response, pointing the way toward truly tailored cancer therapy.

item.title: Two to Three Cups of Coffee a Day Linked to Better Mental Health, Major Study Finds
item.description: A large UK study of 460,000 participants found that drinking two to three cups of coffee a day was associated with improved mental health outcomes, especially in men, likely due to caffeine's anti-inflammatory effects on the brain. Published in the Journal of Affective Disorders, the findings add to growing evidence linking moderate coffee consumption to reduced risk of depression. Researchers noted the benefit appeared with both caffeinated and, to a lesser extent, decaffeinated coffee.

item.title: WHO: 567 Million More People Now Covered by Essential Health Services
item.description: The World Health Organization's latest Results Report found that 567 million additional people gained access to essential health services without facing catastrophic out-of-pocket costs in 2025 — an increase of 136 million in a single year. The figure spans maternal care, vaccines, disease treatment, and primary care across low- and middle-income countries. Director-General Dr. Tedros called it proof that coordinated global health investment delivers real, measurable results for real people.

item.title: 6,500 Volunteers Build 10,000 Beds in 24 Hours for Kids Without One
item.description: In Charlotte, North Carolina, 6,500 volunteers from Lowe's, Bank of America, and the Charlotte Hornets assembled 10,027 wooden beds in a single 24-hour blitz — using 200 miles of lumber and nearly 730,000 screws. The beds, built for Sleep in Heavenly Peace charity, will be distributed across 36 states to children who currently sleep on the floor. More than 140,000 children in the US remain on the charity's waiting list for a bed of their own.

item.title: Book Fairies Are Giving 25,000 Free Books to New York City's Children
item.description: ThriftBooks partnered with literacy nonprofit Book Fairies to donate 25,000 children's books to under-resourced communities across New York City and Long Island, matching titles to reading levels and subjects. The books will flow through a network of Title I schools, community organisations, and educators serving children in the city's most under-served neighbourhoods. Since 2012, Book Fairies has now surpassed 6 million books distributed, reaching its next milestone one donation at a time.

item.title: England's School Menus Will Ditch Daily Treats and Deep-Fried Food by 2027
item.description: England's Department for Education announced new school food standards eliminating daily sugary treats, banning deep-fried options, and limiting grab-and-go meals from September 2027. Hailed as the most significant school nutrition update in a generation, the standards aim to reverse childhood obesity rates and improve concentration and learning. Over 3.5 million children eat school meals every day in England — meaning the change will reshape how millions of young people fuel their bodies and minds.

item.title: London's Long-Awaited V&A East Museum Opens in Stratford
item.description: The Victoria & Albert Museum's eagerly awaited East London branch opened on April 18, bringing world-class design, art, and culture to the Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park in Stratford. Its inaugural exhibition, 'The Music Is Black: A British Story,' traces 125 years of Black music-making in Britain through instruments, fashion, and photography. Two free permanent galleries showcase over 500 objects spanning global art, architecture, and fashion — free to all visitors.

item.title: UNESCO Names Morocco's Rabat World Book Capital on World Book Day
item.description: On April 23 — UNESCO's World Book and Copyright Day — the organisation celebrated Rabat, Morocco as World Book Capital 2026, honouring the city's rich literary heritage and vibrant publishing culture. The designation, the 26th in UNESCO's history, kicks off a year-long programme spotlighting African and Arabic literary traditions and promoting reading for all. Rabat joins a global lineage of book capitals, from Madrid in 2001 to Guadalajara and Athens, that have used the title to deepen public engagement with books.

item.title: The 'Granborghini Project' Takes Lonely Elderly People on Supercar Joyrides
item.description: Mark Cody launched the Granborghini Project in the UK after his grandmother died from loneliness, teaming up with Ferrari and McLaren owners to give elderly residents the thrill of a lifetime. Participants like 82-year-old Betty Tynan, who runs social lunches for isolated seniors, say the rides have 'made our lives' — with many stepping out of the car feeling 'like a rock star' or '20 years younger.' The project tackles what Cody calls a 'widespread epidemic of loneliness among retirees,' one joyful drive at a time.

item.title: Dodgers Smash Franchise Record With 42 Home Runs in First 22 Games
item.description: The Los Angeles Dodgers set a new franchise record by hitting 42 home runs in their first 22 games of the 2026 season, rocketing to a 16-6 record — the best in baseball. The defending back-to-back World Series champions lead the league in home runs, batting average, on-base percentage, and opponent average. Analysts are calling it one of the most dominant early-season starts in MLB history.

item.title: Wembanyama Scores 35 in Playoff Debut, Lighting Up a New Era for the Spurs
item.description: Victor Wembanyama announced himself on the playoff stage in spectacular fashion, scoring 35 points on 13-of-21 shooting in the San Antonio Spurs' 111-98 Game 1 win over Portland — the most points by any Spur in a playoff debut, surpassing Tim Duncan's 32. He also set the record for first-half scoring in an NBA playoff debut going back to 1997. For a franchise that went seven years without the postseason, the victory felt like the dawn of an extraordinary new chapter.

item.title: Edmonton Oilers Open Their Playoff Run With a Dramatic 4-3 Home Victory
item.description: The Edmonton Oilers launched their quest for a third consecutive Stanley Cup Final appearance with a 4-3 home win over the Anaheim Ducks in Game 1 of the NHL playoffs, igniting a packed arena and rekindling some of hockey's most passionate fandom. The win highlighted the Oilers' continued emergence as one of the NHL's premier playoff franchises, built around Connor McDavid and a deep, battle-tested roster. For fans in Edmonton, the night was a reminder that something special is happening in oil country.

item.title: Africa's Tech Startups Raise Record $382M in Q1 2026, Up 35%
item.description: African technology startups secured $382 million in funding in Q1 2026 — a 35% jump from the same period in 2025 and the continent's strongest opening quarter on record. Egypt led with $190 million, followed by South Africa and Kenya. The milestone signals Africa's startup ecosystem entering a mature phase, with Japanese investors joining traditional US and European backers as confidence in the continent's innovation potential soars.

item.title: Apple Reports Record 30% Recycled Materials — Plastic Fully Eliminated From Packaging
item.description: Apple's 2026 Environmental Progress Report revealed a record 30% of material across all products shipped in 2025 came from recycled content, including 100% recycled cobalt in every battery and rare earth elements in every magnet. The company fully eliminated plastic from all packaging, diverted 600,000+ metric tons of waste from landfills, and cut greenhouse gas emissions by over 60% since 2015 — cementing its place as a corporate sustainability benchmark.

item.title: Paraguay Posts South America's Fastest Economic Growth — 6.6% in 2025
item.description: Paraguay's GDP grew 6.6% in 2025, the fastest expansion in 12 years and the strongest performance in South America for the second consecutive year, driven by a 10.5% surge in agriculture and 10.9% growth in hydroelectric energy. The IMF upgraded the country's 2026 outlook and Moody's awarded Paraguay its first-ever investment-grade credit rating, placing this landlocked nation firmly on the map for global investors.

item.title: Breakthrough Prize Honours Scientists Who Restored Sight to Children Born Blind
item.description: Scientists Jean Bennett, Katherine High, and Albert Maguire received the $3 million Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences for developing Luxturna — the first FDA-approved gene replacement therapy for inherited retinal disease. Children who previously could only perceive bright light can now attend regular schools and play outdoors at night, marking the first time a single gene correction has permanently restored sight in people born with inherited blindness.

item.title: Moringa 'Miracle Tree' Removes 98% of Microplastics From Drinking Water
item.description: Researchers at São Paulo State University found that seed extract from the moringa tree removes over 98% of PVC microplastics from water, outperforming aluminum sulfate across a wider pH range. The plant-based solution is biodegradable, non-toxic, and carbon-sequestering — a quiet revolution in clean water access for communities worldwide. The study, published in ACS Omega, has drawn global attention for its elegant, nature-derived simplicity.

item.title: Muon g-2 Wins Breakthrough Prize for Most Precise Subatomic Measurement in History
item.description: The Muon g-2 collaboration at Fermilab, CERN, and Brookhaven received the $3 million Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics for measuring the muon's magnetic moment to 127 parts per billion — 30,000 times more precise than the first attempt in 1965. The result offers the most tantalising experimental hint yet that undiscovered particles or forces may govern our universe, pointing toward new physics beyond the Standard Model.

item.title: UK Renewables Top 52% for Second Year — 2025 Was First Year With Zero Coal
item.description: Renewable energy generated a record 52.5% of UK electricity in 2025, the second consecutive year above 50%. Wind contributed 30% of total generation and solar hit a new high of 20 TWh. Most remarkably, 2025 was the first year in over 140 years with zero coal-fired electricity in Britain — a milestone RenewableUK called proof that renewables are now the backbone of the nation's power system.

item.title: India Crosses Historic 150 GW Solar Milestone With Record Annual Additions
item.description: India's cumulative solar capacity surpassed 150 gigawatts after adding a record 44.6 GW in fiscal year 2026 — an 87% year-on-year increase and the fastest any nation has ever bridged a 50 GW solar increment, achieved in just 14 months. PM Modi's Surya Ghar rooftop scheme benefited roughly 2.6 million homes, and India is now the world's third-largest solar market, on track to become the second-largest by end of 2026.

item.title: All Six 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize Winners Are Women — A Historic First
item.description: For the first time in its 37-year history, the Goldman Environmental Prize honoured six women from six continents. Winners stopped a mega mine in Alaska, secured Rio Tinto's first mining accountability in Papua New Guinea, halted fracking in Colombia, and won a landmark UK Supreme Court ruling on fossil fuels. Nigeria's Iroro Tanshi was celebrated for protecting endangered bats and securing 27,000 community livelihoods at Afi Mountain.

item.title: Chile Becomes the First Country in the Americas to Eliminate Leprosy
item.description: The World Health Organization officially verified Chile as leprosy-free in March 2026 — the first nation in the Americas and only the second globally after Jordan to achieve this milestone. Chile has recorded no locally transmitted cases in over 30 years, the result of decades of sustained surveillance, early diagnosis, and political commitment. WHO and PAHO praised the achievement as a global model for disease elimination.

item.title: US Traffic Deaths Drop 6.7% in 2025, Second-Lowest Rate in Over a Century
item.description: The US Department of Transportation announced that an estimated 36,640 people died in traffic crashes in 2025 — a 6.7% drop from 2024 and the second-lowest fatality rate (1.10 per 100 million miles) in over 104 years of recordkeeping. Deaths declined in 39 states, the District of Columbia, and Puerto Rico, reflecting years of investment in road safety technology, infrastructure, and evidence-based enforcement.

item.title: Scientists Map the Entire Nerve Network of the Clitoris for the First Time
item.description: Researchers at Amsterdam University Medical Center used synchrotron X-rays to create the world's first high-resolution 3D map of every nerve pathway in the human clitoris, overturning decades of incomplete anatomical understanding. The breakthrough provides a critical surgical roadmap for gender-affirming procedures and reconstructive surgery for the estimated 230 million survivors of female genital mutilation, marking a landmark advance in women's health science.

item.title: Rwanda Opens Visa-Free Travel to All 55 African Union Member States
item.description: Rwanda has extended full visa-free access to citizens of all 55 African Union member states, allowing 30-day stays without prior authorisation, joined by Ghana, Kenya, Tanzania, Seychelles, Benin, and The Gambia. The policy is already driving measurable growth in cross-border arrivals and hospitality investment. Analysts credit the initiative with accelerating AfCFTA economic integration and positioning East Africa as the continent's most open travel destination.

item.title: US Adult Smoking Rate Falls Below 10% for the First Time in Recorded History
item.description: CDC data confirmed the adult cigarette smoking rate in the United States dropped to 9.9% in 2024 — the first time in recorded history it has fallen to single digits, down from a peak of 42.4% in 1965. The 77% decline over six decades reflects the combined power of taxation, advertising bans, cessation programmes, and generational shifts in social norms — one of modern public health's greatest sustained achievements.

item.title: Zimbabwe's Vaccine Cyclists Pedal Through Elephant Country to Protect 22,000 Girls
item.description: Community health workers in rural Zimbabwe are cycling door-to-door through heat, thorns, and elephant country to deliver HPV vaccines to girls far beyond clinic reach. Backed by UNICEF and the Clinton Health Access Initiative, the programme has immunised over 22,000 girls in Mashonaland West Province — a joyful act of prevention against a disease that kills two-thirds of Zimbabwean women diagnosed with cervical cancer.

item.title: Robert Plant Honoured as Record Store Day Legend at World's Oldest Record Shop
item.description: Led Zeppelin frontman Robert Plant was named the third-ever Record Store Day Legend — after Elton John and Johnny Marr — in a ceremony at Spillers Records in Cardiff, the world's oldest record shop. On April 18, thousands of independent record stores worldwide celebrated Record Store Day's 19th year with exclusive vinyl releases and in-store performances, with Plant declaring that record shops have always been a part of his life.

item.title: San Francisco Art Fair Dedicates Entire 2026 Programme to AAPI Artists
item.description: The 2026 San Francisco Art Fair at Fort Mason dedicated its entire programming to Asian American and Pacific Islander artists, partnering with the Asian Art Museum and Stanford's AAPI Art Initiative. Featuring 90 galleries, participatory performances, and a preview of work headed to the 2026 Venice Biennale, the four-day event celebrated the Bay Area's immigrant legacies and amplified voices that have long shaped but rarely headlined the contemporary art world.

item.title: 92nd Drama League Awards Celebrate a Diverse and Joyful Broadway Season
item.description: The Drama League announced nominees for its 92nd annual awards on April 20, honouring a broad Broadway season featuring Schmigadoon!, Titanique, Ragtime, and Chess. Distinguished Performance nominees include Daniel Radcliffe, Ayo Edebiri, Laurie Metcalf, and Lea Michele. The ceremony, set for May 15 at the Ziegfeld Ballroom, caps what critics are calling one of the most culturally vibrant Broadway years in a decade.

item.title: John Korir Shatters Boston Marathon Course Record With 2:01:52
item.description: Kenyan runner John Korir won his second consecutive Boston Marathon on April 20, obliterating the previous course record of 2:03:02 with a time of 2:01:52 — the fifth-fastest marathon ever run. Three finishers in the same race surpassed the old record, making it the fastest field in the event's 130-year history. Korir and his brother Wesley are now the only sibling duo to have both won the iconic race.

item.title: Sharon Lokedi Defends Boston Marathon Title, Clocks Fourth-Fastest Women's Time Ever
item.description: Kenya's Sharon Lokedi successfully defended her Boston Marathon crown on April 20, winning in 2:18:51 — the fourth-fastest women's finish in the race's 130-year history — despite forgetting her GPS watch at the start. Her dominant back-to-back victory, secured by nearly a minute over the field, cements Lokedi as one of distance running's defining champions and adds another luminous chapter to Kenya's extraordinary marathon legacy.

item.title: New Zealand Sweeps the Oceania Archery Target Championships on Home Soil
item.description: New Zealand archers dominated the 2026 World Archery Oceania Target Championships in Auckland, winning gold in every individual recurve and compound division. Ben McLean and Julia Harrison won the recurve events, while 54-year-old Lisa Walker claimed compound gold. The championships brought together 83 archers from 10 nations, with New Zealand topping the medal table as fans cheered through windy waterfront conditions.
